# AI and Digital Innovation

Hubble


# Data Science Principles

Agent dashboard.
Split between small medium and large. 
Manual risk score from manual review.
Repayments

There may be typos in the data. - It's possible to use fuzzy matching to do any matching.

 There may be missing data. - It might be possible to extract the user or agency from the email address.

 They might use different letter cases. - Key fields like emails and agency names can be converted to lower case before any analysis is done.

 They might accidentally have whitespace before or after the data. – You can strip whitespace.

 Users might share accounts giving a misleading view of submissions under that account. It might not be possible to do anything directly about this, but we could contact the users/accountancy firms and tell them not to share accounts.

The source data relating to the size of agents might not be that accurate, especially for smaller agencies. This is partly why bands are used rather than a direct number.

There might be issues formatting dates. - Dates can be converted to a standard format.

Donut chart of submissions by accountants. Could see that some small agents are making a lot of returns which is suspicious. 

The line chart showing the number of returns each day is useful to quickly see if there are any spikes or issues with returns. It does show some peaks and troughs, that repeat over the week. This is expected, for example you might expect less returns submitted during the weekend, with more submissions on some weekdays. If for example there were lots of returns submitted on a weekend that might suggest a risk. If there were lots of fraudulent or risky returns submitted then it should show up in the graph with a higher peak than normal. This means we can respond faster in near real time to risks.

The pie chart showing the top agents for submissions is useful to get a handle of who is submitting  most of the returns we are receiving. It extracts the top 7 agents and then groups the rest together by size. One thing that stands out is that there are some small agents submitting a large number of returns. This isn't expected so might suggest a risk.

The boxplot chart shows details about the number of returns submitted broken down by size. This further highlights that there are a couple of outliers in the small agent population, they are very far from even the upper quartile. This further supports the idea that there are some risks with these agents.

The final table, structures the data and extracts the top 10 submitters. This highlights that there are a couple of submitters who are submitting many more returns than normal.

These visualisations make it easier to see and understand what is happening, and they also highlight outliers and areas of risk in a user friendly format.

**Null hypothesis:**

There is no significant difference in the mean risk score for Agent 4 and other small agents.

**agent_4_mean == small_agents_mean**

**Alternative hypothesis:**

There is a significant difference between the mean risk score for Agent 4 than the other small agents.

**agent_4_mean != small_agents_mean**

## Choosing statistical significance test

·       There are two independent groups.

·       Distributions are normal

·       n > 30.

·       We don’t know the population standard deviation.

·       Ratio data.

Want to compare the means of two independent groups. Both distributions are normal and n is very high, >30. We don't know the population standard deviation, so we can use an independent T test instead of Z test.

We aren’t testing more than two groups, so aren’t using ANOVA. We aren’t using categorical variables so not using Chi-Squared. The samples are independent so not using paired T-test.

The type of T-test depends on if the variances are the same, we can use a Fstat test for this.

Fstat = small_agents_std**2 / agent_4_std**2= 2.275/0.385 = 5.918

df1= small_agents_size-1 = 52,078

df2= agent_4_size-1 =5,0751

Two tailed Fcrit at 1% significance

Fcrit = stats.f.ppf(1 – 0.01, df1, df2) = 1.0500

Since Fstat > Fcrit, the variances are unequal.

So, we use **Welch’s t-test** since the variances are unequal.

t_stat = (agent_4_mean - small_agents_mean) / np.sqrt((agent_4_std**2 / agent_4_size) + (small_agents_std**2 / small_agents_size))

**Calculated values for risk scores.**

Agent 4 std: 0.6201281489719113

Agent 4 size: 5076

Agent 4 mean: 6.967494089834515

Small agents std: 1.5085528533204475

Small agents size: 52079

Small agents mean: 5.498070239443922

Degrees of freedom: 12221.565387262237

Since the variances are unequal so df is calculated using.

df = (agent_4_std**2/agent_4_size + small_agents_std**2/small_agents_size)**2 / ((agent_4_std**2/agent_4_size)**2/(agent_4_size-1) + (small_agents_std**2/small_agents_size)**2/(small_agents_size-1))

## Statistical Test

We can put those figures into the Welch T-test formula above or use scipy

stats.ttest_ind(agent_4.risk, small_agents.risk, equal_var=False)

To get

t_stat = 134.443

Since we are checking if the meant is different, we are using a two tailed with alpha = 0.01

Since the degrees of freedom are higher than in the tables we use scipy to calculate the critical value.

t_crit = stats.t.ppf(1 – 0.01/2, df) = 2.5762

This value lines up with the higher degrees of freedom values in the T-tables and the Z-table.

Since t_stat > t_crit, we reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative hypothesis. Since we used a high 99% confidence level, we can be confident that the null hypothesis is wrong.  

The mean risk score for agent 4 is higher than the mean risk score for small agents, and this difference is statistically significant. So, agent 4 is a high risk agent. This means that we might want to put more effort into reviewing returns from this agent and take appropriate action. We might want to review other data from this agent in more detail, such as if they are asking for a repayment at a higher rate than normal. I would run more tests over other variables to see exactly what is different and what is similar to the small agent population.

There are various factors to take into account, what are the legal requirements, ethics and morality. There can be legal requirements which apply more generally to everyone in the country. Organisations might have their own ethical rules and systems that just apply to that organisation. Some organisations have systems in place to help ensure that the organisation complies with legal requirements and more.

The fundamental ethical question is about how the freedoms and well-being of individuals be balanced against the security or economic interests of society. Data can be used to fight terrorism, improve medicine, support public policy research, fight crime, detect fraud, assess credit risk, etc. But we need to balance the use against the rights of individuals.

A key piece of legislation to protect individuals is the General Data Protection Regulation(GDPR) which is implemented through the Data Protection Act(DPA) 2018. The main principles are

·       Lawfulness, fairness and transparency

·       Purpose limitation

·       Data minimisation

·       Accuracy

·       Storage limitation

·       Integrity and confidentiality

·       Accountability

There is also legislation around surveillance by government in the Investigatory Powers Act(IPA) 2016 and Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act(RIPA) 2000. This covers typical surveillance but in the modern times it can also cover online investigations and surveillance of people. E.g. if you are regularly checking someone’s facebook account, and making notes that might be covered by the legislation.

Two organisations which have oversight are the Information Commissioner’s Office(ICO), who provide oversight over data protection. They provide guidance over GDPR, can provide support and advice and also step in when people aren’t following the legislation.

The other is the Office for National Statistics, who review how data collected by the government is used.

Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development(OECD) have eight key principles.

·       Collection limitation principle,

·       Data quality principle

·       Purpose specification principle

·       Use limitation principle

·       Security safeguards principle

·       Openness principle

·       Individual participation principle

·       Accountability principle.

The proposed system collects a number of data sets.

When a user interacts via the web, the user should easily know what data is being collected about them through a banner or other means. Data like IP addresses are treated as personal data. We should only be collecting data for a specific purpose. Here we would use it to identify risks and fraud. While we are looking at fraud we should limit it to what data we require and need to balance it with the rights of people. Details like the IP address can be used to identify fraud by a single person using different personas, so collecting such data is justified.

The data collected is stored in S3 buckets, which should ensure integrity. It would be important to ensure everything is setup and configured in a secure manner. So, this would be with everything with a virtual private cloud(VPC), with limited secure connections to the internet. Where appropriate data should be encrypted at rest and in transit.

Also, the Lake Formation service should be used to control access to data. So, users can only access the data they have a business need for. Also we want to be monitoring access to ensure that internal users are only accessing the data they have a business need to. E.g. we would want to flag users who are looking up a footballer’s details for personal interest. We also want to use this to identify any usage that is unusual, which could help identify any breaches.

There should be clear data retention policies for the Lakehouse to comply with GDPR.

The ML over the data might be used to help categorise data and make it easier to analyse and identify items. But we shouldn’t be making automated decisions using ML.

Potential concerns around machine learning(ML) include

How models could enable or increase bias. This be because if the training data is biased the models would be biased as well. Examples of this are where ML was used to set bail amounts but was found to set higher levels of bail for coloured people. We need to review the data first to ensure that the training data isn’t biased. You may also want to exclude protected characteristics from the training data, so that it’s not possible to use certain characteristics when modelling. This might also include combinations of data that might be a proxy for a certain characteristics, like the address and income which have been used as a proxy for race.

Flawed decisions. There may be an overreliance on ML, which might give misleading outputs, especially if there isn’t enough supporting data or it’s not of the right type. There should be analysis of what data is used and if it includes the right data for the outcomes we are looking at. There can be various test/learn datasets, such that you can test the models on unseen data. This might just be by splitting up the original data set, but also might be over time, where you train over multiple years(not including last year) but test again the previous year.

ML and AI could result in a reduction in jobs. There may be some uncertainty especially in the short terms as things change. But it also opens up more opportunities and allows people to be more productive if they use those tools. So, a project that previously would require a small team could potentially be done by a single person using the latest ML and AI.

# Disruptive Leadership for Sustainable Strategy

No assessment

# Machine Learning using Cloud Computing

Cloud computing AWS system

Amazon Web Services(AWS) provide a variety of Machine Learning(ML) services (_Choosing an AWS machine learning service,_ 2024), from low level infrastructure to fully managed services.

EC2 instances are virtual servers that provide greater user control. There are Deep Learning Open Source Software(OSS) stacks which come with key ML software such as TensorFlow and/or PyTorch preinstalled, making it easy to get up and running (_AWS Deep Learning AMI (Amazon Linux 2)_, 2025). Nvidia based GPU systems are often recommended for ML (_Recommended GPU Instances - Deep Learning AMI_, no date) due CUDA and widespread support. They can run ML loads much faster than CPUs. AWS also have their own Trainium chips for training and Inferentia chips for deployment of models (_Amazon EC2 Inf2 instances_, no date). They can have 70% lower cost for inferencing (_Amazon EC2 Inf1 Instances_, no date) and 30-40% lower cost for training (_AWS Trainium_, no date) but this depends on the workload.

One system used for developing ML systems is SageMaker which has a number of tools you can use (_Amazon SageMaker,_ 2025) (figure 1 below).


![A screenshot of a computer
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1 Overview of SageMaker(Amazon SageMaker, 2025)

JupyterLab and RStudio are tools commonly used in data analytics and ML. JupyterLab can be configured so that when it comes to training that can be scaled up and done over many EC2 instances(figure 2 below), which is much more efficient than manually firing up and closing EC2 instances. SageMaker also has MLflow which can helps manage and track ML experiments.

![A screenshot of a computer program
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 2 Scalable training from [https://sagemaker.readthedocs.io/en/stable/frameworks/tensorflow/using_tf.html](https://sagemaker.readthedocs.io/en/stable/frameworks/tensorflow/using_tf.html)

There are API based tools like Textract which can streamline the process of extracting text.

There are no/low-code tools which increases accessibility to non-experts. AWS Rekognition lets you fine-tune using your own images and labels via GUI. AWS Comprehend lets you train custom text classification models via GUI. Canvas is a GUI method of using and training ML (figure 3 below).

![A screenshot of a computer
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)


Figure 3 Canvas GUI text classification

Cloudwatch can monitor and log various aspects, like CPU, memory and GPU usage, which can be particularly important around the efficiency and use of the ML systems.

Company accounts are in a format called iXBRL, a (x)html document where figures are tagged with a fixed taxonomy. This allows for the data to be reliably extracted from the accounts. But the tagging quality can be poor, in some cases over 70% of the figures are untagged. This can make it hard to extract and analyse this data. Descriptions and their values can otherwise be extracted but descriptions can vary greatly making profiling across them onerous and error prone. The application will categorize the descriptions based on the iXBRL taxonomy.

In the past methods such as simple cosine similarity across descriptions and naïve bayes have poor results and using more complicated LLM methods like BERT have performance issues. A bespoke deep neural network(DNN) is envisioned to provide the best of both, with good quality results with good performance.

Using ODC we could start an EC2 instance with a GPU to do the training, but inference would be done on an EC2 instance with only a CPU. This is due to the cost of GPU instances meaning we can’t keep it running all the time and that it would take over 10 minutes to fully setup a GPU instance, and for most small jobs it could already be done using CPU. The EC2 instance could access the documents stored in a S3 bucket (figure 4 below)

![A diagram of a company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company's company'
AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1 Architecture 

To create custom DNNs in an efficient manner the Keras framework of TensorFlow in a Jupyter notebook will be used. Initially a simple DNN will be used, and then more complicated NN such as Recurrent Neural Networks(RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks(CNN) will be tried, which should capture more data around the structure of phrases.

This will allow users to profile across data more reliably even when the data is not tagged, replacing long and complicated text matching. This would result in clearer, higher quality profiles that would have benefits of helping target high risk companies for tax investigations increasing tax revenue and reduce investigations into low-risk companies, reducing customer burden.

A ML pipeline was used to develop, train and evaluate the model (figure 8 below).

![Illustration of an example machine learning pipeline as described in the text.](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 1 Example machine learning pipeline, from Machine learning pipelines. Deep learning frameworks. from: https://app.qa.com/course/topic-4-deep-learning-frameworks-ai-1698/machine-learning-pipelines/?context_id=15077&context_resource=lp
